speaker: interviewer
text: Today is January 17, 1997. The survivor is Harry Konar. We are in Riviera Beach, Florida. And this is tape number two.

speaker: interviewee
text: Once the Germans started to occupied us, our country, it took time. By intimidation or whatever other reasons, they knocked you down. Or they took you out and assembled a few hundred people, and many times I was one of them, for no reasons at all. Let you stand there for 10 hours-- outside. Nothing. You couldn't go to the bathroom. You couldn't do anything. They took away the self-dignity from us. And then it started until they got organized, because it took between the occupying us and they occupied Warsaw was four weeks, until they got organized and they knew what they were going to do. I don't think they had plans then. And then they started recruiting people to go to work. And a lot of people volunteered because we didn't have no food. Even though it wasn't a ghetto yet, the first approximately six or eight months, but still there was shortages because the farmers wouldn't come in to market. But we managed. We didn't have everything. And one incident happened. It was 1940, in April. And my mother had prepared food for Pesach. And some Pole told them that the rabbi was living on our floor-- there were three apartments on our floor, and he's been there for years, even before we moved in-- and he must have had something to do with them. Old, elderly people and so on. Well, they knocked on their door and they didn't answer. And they knocked on our door. I was home alone. They came in and there was pilots out for Sunday something to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Pilots?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the division. You know, flyers. They came in, maybe 10 or 20, emptied out-- my father had a complete set of [NON-ENGLISH], of Talmud books, and they thought they were in the rabbi's house when they saw all the Bibles. A great big section with all the books. Threw it all out the window, built a fire downstairs, and burned all the stuff. And then they threw out all the food we used to hide. We didn't have refrigeration then, but it was in the winter time. April was still cold for I know the guards were still frozen. There was snow outside. They threw out all the food through the window and burned everything. On top of it, I was beaten up. I saw a picture of myself trying to escape up to another floor, but I never could find it anymore, which some of the Germans took. Next morning, the people outside didn't know what to do, so they called-- they went to the Jewish commander, to the Jewish office-- which they had established-- to come. And finally they left. The next morning my mother was called to the courthouse, which was a German judge, and the head of the-- president of Jewish office. And my mother happened to recognize, because she wasn't home, she came in later, that she recognized some of the pilots, pointed them out. They denied it, and for this the town was assessed another-- I don't remember the amount, 30,000 of 50,000 Polish zlotys, or 100,000, that we had to pay, because we accuse Germans of doing stuff like this. I mean literally, they took the dignity and everything away from you.

speaker: interviewer
text: But these were not the SS?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they were not SS. They were strictly from the flyers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Air force.

speaker: interviewee
text: Air force. And a Sunday afternoon something to do. Beat up on the Jews. And it's gone back that's what my cousin was telling me that that's what happened in Germany. This-- this is a lot of baloney that they tell you-- only the SS did it. It took a lot of other ones. And I'll tell you another fact-- civilians to participate-- otherwise, they couldn't have never accomplished. Of course, it was much easier for them on Polish territory to do it because the Poles are anti-Semites from way back. This was going on, and then the end of '40 they started the ghetto, which meant Jews lived in a 10-kilometer area they squeeze us into two or three. My sister and her husband and child moved in with us. We didn't have large quarters to start with, but we brought in another family. But we managed. In '41, we realized-- one morning we woke up we saw a lot of artillery, bus, trucks, and horse and wagon moving towards the Russian front. Right away-- I don't remember the month when that-- right away we knew that they were attacking Russia. And it turned out in a few weeks later. Now there we heard more cruel stories, shortly thereafter, of what the Ukrainians, the Lithuanians did to the Jewish people. And a lot of our people who escaped-- younger people, before the Germans came in, escaped towards Russia-- were caught in this melee. And it was terrible in there, so we understood. But we were sitting in Radom. We didn't know. We didn't see anything, it didn't affect us, but otherwise nobody told you anything what was happening. A few months later, it's also in '41, one night they came down. They were looking for communists. Came with a list of names. It was past midnight. I went to the door and there was the SS. I should show them where so-and-so lives. So I took him up to the third floor, fourth floor, and show them. We didn't have no idea what it was. We found out later that they were communists, and so on. I knew that they were not Orthodox with us. In those days, I didn't know much the difference, communism and so on. I took him up. Then about 15 or half hour later the same SS man came back to the door. I didn't go anymore. My mother went to the door. He wanted me. And I don't know what she said, but he left me alone. The next morning, 5 o'clock or 6 o'clock, we heard cries. They took all those people, about 100 or 200, I don't know exactly. Took them downstairs and shot them, every one of them, with a silencer. If you were the right person or not, as long as the name matched, it's all that mattered. They shot a friend of mine who was a yeshiva boy just like me, but his name happened to be Perl, and lived right around the corner from us. Just to give you an example of the names of people that I knew. Some of them maybe were communist, probably were from before the war. They just killed them.

speaker: interviewer
text: There were 100 people living above you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no,  no-- from the whole city, from the whole ghetto. No, no. Not from us, sorry. From the whole area. We saw later, about 9, 10 o'clock we were out on the street, and we saw the wagons going by with the dead people to the cemetery. You know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you actually see them shoot them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, nobody did. But I saw the bodies the next morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did they shoot them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right going down the stairs they left everyone at the bottom of the stairs. They took them out of the apartment house, walked him down, and with a silencer shot him.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the stairs? Or down at the bottom?

speaker: interviewee
text: Down at the bottom of the stairs.

speaker: interviewer
text: But so nobody could see.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody could see, and nobody could hear. And I think we didn't know until in the morning some people went out to daven or maybe they went out to work or whatever, and it's from we heard the screams. That's what woked us up when they stepped over.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how many were in your building do you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of how many they shot in our building? Maybe three or four. Well actually, it was an L-shaped building. So-- at our entrance, it was either one or two. I don't recall. And the other entrances-- like I said it was a tall building, three or four stories high-- and on each floor only had three or two apartments. So they picked and they just shot them and it was that it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was all over the city.

speaker: interviewee
text: All over the city. They went with a list. They went with a list. This-- this I saw. I saw the list I didn't know all the names, but I knew the people who lived upstairs it was a son, or son-in-law that lived there. Then the ghetto start getting smaller and smaller. They brought people in from smaller villages, but yet they put them up and we had a big shul which here would be considered conservative, and which could accommodate 500 to 1,000 people. They probably put 2,000 in there. They brought them in from a little village cleaned out and put them all in there to live under those conditions. The beis medrash the same way. The other places of worship were not so affected because they were small. I think I mentioned to you the place that we used to go to was the rabbi's house. They only accommodated maybe 30 or 40 people. So they just closed up and that was it. There was no place to go to daven during the ghetto an so on. And if there was, you didn't take a chance. You were afraid that somebody will turn on you or whatever. There was always somebody against it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you get food?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were able to get out-- we had rations, first of all, ration cards or whatever. But we were able to go out, as I explained to you, like I used to go out with covering up. And my mother-- it was easier for a woman to get out of the ghetto than a man, and a mother was able to go to the farmers or to people she has known and pick up food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was it easier for her?

speaker: interviewee
text: A woman they didn't bother so much as a man, for some reason. I don't know why.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she hide her--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah, you had to hide it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You each wore one.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were regular dressed. We didn't have prison garb yet. Regular clothes, but we had the armband. Was supposed to wear it up here, but you slid it down here and wore a coat over it. And then we had to go out to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was on that band?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a Star of David with the word Jude on it, just like in my passport there.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you covered it up when you went out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not-- when I went to work, I didn't. When I went to work for them, I think I mentioned we all had to go out to work. If you find your own job where to go to, which was much better than just every day being recruited to something different and you ended up you didn't know where. And we were fortunate. Some of us, at first we volunteered to be gardeners and so on, and we grew vegetables, we grew flowers for the German, for the Wehrmacht, for the army. And was much easier than work for the SS. Then I think I mentioned I saw a-- a soldier came down, a Wehrmacht soldier came down. It was a big place, large place with a lot of grass. He came down to feed the horse, you know, for a few hours. So I went up to him and asked him if he needed help. And that I know my way around horses, and he gladly accepted me, and I get the job. And I used to go every morning early to the stable to clean the horse with him and do everything else that's necessary. And then about 8 o'clock, whatever, the officers would come to ride and the horse was ready. In the meantime I cleaned up the stalls and so on. But at the same time, I got enough food for myself, and to take home, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: From whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: From him, from the soldiers. The Wehrmacht, some of them were bad, but some of them were terrible. So you avoided the bad ones and like they had a mess hall where they ate, and if there was leftover, there were some other people, a friend of mine who worked cleaning the barracks, or cleaning the houses, or so on, then we would be in line last to eat the leftovers. But at least we had something to eat. We had food.

speaker: interviewer
text: But other than that you got food by what way?

speaker: interviewee
text: We used to have ration cards, first of all. My mother had some money which she sold some of the leather left. When the war started, after a month or two she took the whole leather and put it up in the-- in the factory. It wasn't ours. It was a Polish factory. And this happened in '41. Then one day the man calls us from the factory, [NON-ENGLISH], his name was, that they're coming to confiscate all the leather. It's going to be shipped to Germany. We went out there, we slept, we had a garden hut out there, so my brother and my mother and I, we slept there. And at night we carried the leather out from the factory through the house in there. I even got sick afterwards. I don't know what I did. And in the morning we took one of those coaches which you were still able to do. It wasn't a ghetto yet at this time. And we took it home. Not all of it, but some of it. But the main thing was that the man who came to confiscate all this leather, I saw him, I know his name. He doesn't live anymore. A men by the name of Freudenberg, from Weinheim, Germany. And he did it to everybody-- to all the factories. It didn't make any difference, Jews and non-Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no. No, Freudenberg was a-- a large family. They were one of the-- now I understand they were one of the biggest in Europe in the leather business, and it was his job. I don't think he was a Nazi. It didn't make a difference. They went around to Europe to confiscate the leather. They confiscated like everything else to take back to Germany. But the fact that I saw him. He knew I was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: One brother was Walter. And I don't know if he was one. One plus a professor at the Heidelberg school. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He's the one I'm talk about more later.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had, by selling the leather, by this place-- we had some money. For money you can always buy something. Who did you sell the leather to?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Polish people. Everybody did something to survive. Life is so-- you become resourceful when you need be.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you smuggled it out, and you got something. If I needed a pair of shoes to wear, I would go to somebody I knew and I got a coupon, which you had to have, you know, and I got a pair of shoes. There was all things-- But you had to pay something.

speaker: interviewee
text: Something. But not much. Because the problem wasn't the money, the problem was that you couldn't get anything. The money was worthless. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When we were-- not to jump it, when we were on the train, the Poles would come to the train and sell you a potato, or an onion for 500 zlotys. You know? It was strictly-- they knew what they were doing. They knew we had money-- some of us did, not everybody had the money to do it. But 500 zlotys for potato, just something to eat, not even a piece of bread. If you had it, you did it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was going on for some time, until we saw too many villages, and we didn't know exactly what was happening, that they were being emptied out. Like my [NON-ENGLISH]-- one day my grandfather showed up, and he said there's nobody left. So this is the time I took him to Warsaw to his son. It was easier for him to come to us because we would right across the Vistula. Then I took him to his son in Warsaw, and that's when I traveled, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] did something.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then my aunt, the one I have the picture with the five kids, got a message to us that they were starving. There was no food. They took the whole village out and they put them to a town called Otwock, which is near Warsaw and was a gathering point. But we didn't know what it was. But it was a gathering point for later months to get them all into Treblinka.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went down and took him some food. And while I came to Warsaw, I had to change trains. I took everything and put it into a locker and went outside-- because I knew the town so well. And then all of a sudden a Pole hollers, there goes a Jew!

speaker: interviewee
text: I run back in. I knew what would happen. He would have sold me for a bottle of vodka. I run back in, I run in the train and forgot about it. Those little things that-- that happen in order to survive. And then one day I told you I worked with the horses. And there was a thing-- by then we already knew-- I'm jumping ahead before our liquidation I had to tell you this because it's-- The liquidations started approximately in June '42, when they came down one morning and they, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. Everybody out. And there was the first train, maybe 4,000 or 8,000. We don't even know how many. And they took them and within 24 hours they were gone to Treblinka. And my mother was on the first train. And my sister-- my brother-in-law worked in the ammunition factory, and he saw what was going on, that they were going to be shipped out too, he came back and thought he can go over with his wife and child. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] At this point, we already lived with the Wehrmacht.

speaker: interviewer
text: You and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Myself and my brother, one brother. The other brother was in the ammunition factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: The youngest one.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: William.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, Morris was with me. He was in the ammunition factory. Then two weeks later, they did the second liquidation that they liquidate the ghetto from-- from 30,000 people down to 5,0000 or 2,000, you know, I don't-- and they made it very small. And this was all people that they needed, craftsman or whatever it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then one day I was going out. I had the advantage of being with the horse-- I could go any place I wanted to, because the horse was my passport. One day I came down, I had to go to shoe the horse. And I came down, I stopped off at the small ghetto and the captain there, the-- whoever he was-- says to me, I wonder who is free to go alone, you or the horse?

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't go alone. It's the horse that was free to go. I was with the horse, I was safe. Alone, I would have been shot. That's the way life was for a while. Then one morning we were right near a railroad track, because there was another group of Jewish people who worked on a loading barrels of oil or gas. It was a station there right next to ours-- depot, or whatever it was. And I saw a train stopped, and for some reason it was a hot day and was Kippur day.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yom Kipper-- and we still fasted then. It was about 1 or 2 o'clock in if afternoon, and the doors opened up and there were the Hasidim. Everybody was a Hasid then, with the white kittle, and with beards and so on, some with [NON-ENGLISH]. And I took the horse and walked out so I can talk to them. It gave me a passport. [CRYING] The-- the rabbi said to me, he said, be strong. Eat. Survive. And take nekama.

speaker: interviewer
text: And take what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nekama. Nekama. You know? In Hebrew-- take revenge. And it was a little bit of more than this, but I went into the camp and I told my brothers and everybody, I says, look, there's no reason to fast or do anything. Now let's go and eat and see that we can survive. There's no use to punish yourself. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I told them [CRYING] this, I never forget. A picture like this. Elie Wiesel was in [NON-ENGLISH] a few years ago, and I told my story about this. [CRYING] From then on, we knew what was happening. We didn't know--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was that train going? You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Treblinka. We didn't know what was happening. Everything in our area, from what I understand now, went to Treblinka. One day they came in-- and there was a private company from Leipzig, to disinfect the German barracks, and I was assigned to help him, to shown him around and help him carry the canisters and whatever. And when he was all done, he said to me in German, he said, Hersh, I make it for you very easy. Stay here. Don't go. [CRYING] You'll be gassed right here. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I'll never forget it. This great big German guy, civilian clothes. So when they tell you the stories they didn't know about it-- it was a private company. They knew what was going on. They used to bid on all the material to supply to the crematoriums.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he call you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hersh. My name in German Herschel, so it was Hersh. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We had a-- after two, three days working with him, you know, he was very honest. He says, I'll make it for you very easy. You don't have to go to there. I can do it right here for you.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you say?

speaker: interviewee
text: I looked at him and I don't know if I had tears in my eyes or whatever, and I walked away. I didn't say anything. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But it was a very traumatic experience. Not that I didn't know by then-- I already knew what was happening. But until it hits you yourself, you don't know. You don't know what's happening. Then I think I mentioned to you in 1940, they bought into our camp about 20 or 30 Russian prisoners of war. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And then one day, those guys decided to escape. One of them I think was Jacob, his first name, was Jewish, but he spoke German. And it was an-- very-- it wasn't very common between the Russians to speak German. But they were all-- he was a tank driver, he told me. And they lost their battle at the time. And I helped him-- I got him some clothes, some suits and so on, whatever I could get together, because they wanted to escape in civilian clothes. He didn't want me. I would have gone with him had he asked me, but he didn't want me. He wanted just-- as a matter of fact, there were 20 or 30 of them and just eight or 10 escaped. Because they didn't trust their own even then, some of them. Because some of them cooperated with the Germans, some Russians. As soon as this happened, they took us and put us into the small ghetto, which we called at the time. There we stayed about two months approximately, maybe six weeks, and we wanted to go and work for some place. And there was a place called [NON-ENGLISH]-- it was a supply house which they supplied food for the SS, and for other German outfits I assume-- but mostly for the SS. They put us in there in 1943. And about four months later, the man who was in charge of the German SS men picked me out to work in the food warehouse. Why, I don't know, but he picked me out of the whole group. Well, we had food in there because it was a food warehouse-- that they gave us the leftover, and so on. But they worked us hard. Well, in the food warehouse, of course, was much easier. I worked inside. And I can remember I smuggled out a salami, like down here, tied it with a little string. Walked outside to the outhouse where they were working, and my brother knew, and I tied it inside. I put the nail in, and he came in afterwards and took the salami out. I mean that's it's the things we innovated how to survive.
